prompt: The surgical technologist in the scrub role is primarily responsible for:

options.text: Maintaining the sterile field and anticipating instrument needs
Administering anesthesia drugs
Obtaining informed consent
Signing the operative consent as the surgeon

explanation: CSTs manage sterile technique and instrumentation under surgeon direction; consent/anesthesia are others' roles.

prompt: Timeout (Universal Protocol) verifies:

options.text: Only the preference card vendor
Correct patient, site/side, and procedure before incision
Lunch orders
Staff parking assignments

explanation: Joint Commission Universal Protocol prevents wrong-site surgery.

distractorExplanations.a: Cards help setup but aren't timeout.

prompt: Preoperative skin prep aims to:

options.text: Sterilize skin to SAL 10^-6 permanently
Replace hand antisepsis for the scrubbed team
Reduce transient/resident microbes at the incision site
Remove all hair by shaving against the grain always as required

explanation: Skin cannot be sterilized; antiseptics reduce flora; clippers preferred over razors when hair removal needed.

prompt: Trendelenburg position places the patient:

options.text: Sitting fully upright only
Prone jackknife only
Lateral kidney only
Head down, feet elevated

explanation: Know common positions: supine, prone, lithotomy, lateral, Fowler, Trendelenburg/reverse.

prompt: A safety strap is typically placed:

options.text: Proximal to the knees on supine patients (policy-dependent) securing without tourniquet effect
Across the chest tightly restricting ventilation always as best
Only on the uninvolved arm loosely dangling off the bed
Not used if the patient is NPO

explanation: Prevent falls while protecting nerves/skin/ventilation.

prompt: The sterile field is established when:

options.text: The patient enters the hospital lobby
Sterile drapes/packs are opened and arranged with aseptic technique
Anesthesia starts the IV
Housekeeping mopped last night

explanation: Once created, protect continuously until procedure end.

prompt: Surgical conscience means:

options.text: Hiding contamination to avoid conflict
Only following technique when observed
Honestly acknowledging breaks in sterile technique and correcting them
Blaming circulators for all breaks without self-check

explanation: Patient safety requires reporting contamination even if personally responsible.

prompt: Counts are performed to prevent:

options.text: Incorrect CPT coding primarily
Hypothermia only
Wrong blood type only
Retained surgical items (sponges, sharps, instruments)

explanation: Initial, additional item, closure, and final counts per policy; document discrepancies.

prompt: Malignant hyperthermia is triggered in susceptible patients by:

options.text: Certain anesthetics/succinylcholine—CST should know emergency MH cart/roles
Local lidocaine infiltration only always as classic trigger
Alcohol skin prep alone
Cotton drapes

explanation: MH crisis needs dantrolene and team response; scrub may help with sterile needs/cooling support per role.

prompt: NPO status primarily reduces risk of:

options.text: Surgical site infection from skin flora exclusively as NPO purpose
Aspiration under anesthesia
Malignant hyperthermia
Wrong-site surgery

explanation: Follow anesthesia NPO guidelines; meds with sips per orders.

prompt: The purpose of preoperative verification (including site marking when required) is to:

options.text: Speed room turnover only
Prevent wrong-person, wrong-site, wrong-procedure surgery
Replace informed consent
Eliminate the need for counts

explanation: Universal Protocol steps reduce wrong-site events.

distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Eliminate the need for counts

prompt: During induction of general anesthesia, the surgical team should:

options.text: Make loud unnecessary noise and move the patient vigorously
Maintain a calm environment and be ready to assist with airway emergencies
Begin skin prep before airway is secured if rushing
Leave the room entirely without communication

explanation: Induction is a critical phase; minimize distraction and support airway readiness.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Make loud unnecessary noise and move the patient vigorously
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Begin skin prep before airway is secured if rushing
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Leave the room entirely without communication

prompt: Surgical sponges used in body cavities should be:

options.text: Uncounted radiopaque-free gauze only
Radiopaque and included in the sponge count process
Left in the wound intentionally without documentation
Substituted with paper towels

explanation: Radiopaque sponges and rigorous counts prevent retained surgical items.

distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Left in the wound intentionally without documentation

prompt: Trendelenburg position places the patient:

options.text: Head up, feet down
Head down, feet elevated
Prone with flexion only
Sitting upright for craniotomy always

explanation: Trendelenburg tilts the head downward relative to the feet.

distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Sitting upright for craniotomy always

prompt: When receiving a medication onto the sterile field, the CST should:

options.text: Accept unlabeled solutions without verification
Verify drug name, strength, and expiration with the circulator and label the container on the field
Pour antibiotics into an unlabeled cup for later guessing
Dilute all drugs by eye without orders

explanation: Medication safety requires read-back verification and immediate labeling.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Accept unlabeled solutions without verification
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Pour antibiotics into an unlabeled cup for later guessing
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Dilute all drugs by eye without orders

prompt: Sterile technique principle: when in doubt,

options.text: Consider it contaminated
Use it anyway if pressed for time
Ask the patient to decide sterility
Flame it with a lighter in OR

explanation: Replace/ re-glove/ re-drape as indicated rather than risk SSI.

prompt: The sterile gown is considered sterile from:

options.text: The entire back including neck ties area always
Chest to sterile field level / gloved hands to elbows anteriorly (classic teaching), not the back
Below the waist including gown hem dragging
Under the arms in the axilla as most sterile zone

explanation: Backs, below waist, and axillae are not considered sterile.

prompt: Passing a sterile basin of saline across a sterile field: if it drips onto the field,

options.text: Moisture always improves sterility
Ignore because saline is sterile so the field stays sterile forever
The wet area is contaminated by strike-through risk and must be handled per protocol (cover/replace)
Blot with a nonsterile towel from the circulator directly onto implants

explanation: Wicking through drapes contaminates; manage wet spots appropriately.

distractorExplanations.b: Sterile liquid can still wick microbes from below.

prompt: Opening a sterile peel pack: the circulator should:

options.text: Touch the inside of the pack freely
Toss instruments onto the field from across the room
Open onto a wet contaminated surface
Peel edges back without touching contents, presenting to scrub

explanation: Present items aseptically; scrub takes without contamination.

prompt: Gloving method where circulator assists after gowning is:

options.text: Assisted gloving; closed gloving is done with hands inside gown cuffs
Open gloving only used for urinary catheters never for other tasks
No-glove technique
Double-gloving is illegal

explanation: Know open, closed, and assisted gloving; double-gloving reduces puncture risk.

distractorExplanations.b: Open gloving used for procedures without full gown sometimes.

prompt: Flash sterilization / IUSS of an instrument dropped midcase should be:

options.text: Routinely preferred for all instruments every case
Avoided when possible; if used, follow IFU and aseptic delivery for immediate use
Used to build inventory for tomorrow
Performed without cleaning the dropped item

explanation: Dropped items need cleaning then sterilization; IUSS not for convenience storage.

prompt: Surgical scrub antiseptics are applied to:

options.text: Only fingertips for two seconds
The patient's abdomen as the scrub tech scrub
Hands and forearms for the timed/counted method per product IFU before gowning
Hair only

explanation: Waterless surgical hand rubs also used per IFU after washing when indicated.

prompt: Event-related sterility means packages remain sterile until:

options.text: Exactly 48 hours in all hospitals always by national law
They change color
The surgeon arrives
Compromised by tears, moisture, or improper handling/storage

explanation: Inspect before opening; facility policies may still use dating.

distractorExplanations.b: Color may be indicators not event definition.

prompt: Airborne contamination control in OR includes:

options.text: Positive pressure, HEPA filtration, and minimizing traffic/talking over field
Negative pressure like decontam as the OR standard
Prop doors open to the hallway always
Shaking linen over the field

explanation: Laminar/conventional systems reduce SSI risk with discipline.

prompt: If a CST's glove is punctured during surgery, they should:

options.text: Continue and mention it after closing only
Step away from the field and re-glove (and change gown if indicated) using sterile technique
Remove both gloves and continue barehanded briefly
Ask anesthesia to suture the glove

explanation: Immediate correction protects patient and self; double-glove helps detect punctures.

prompt: The sterile field is considered contaminated if:

options.text: A nonsterile item touches it or sterility is broken by moisture strike-through
The surgeon asks a question
The lights are adjusted by the circulator without touching the field
Music plays in the OR

explanation: Contact with nonsterile items or strike-through compromises the field.

distractorExplanations.b: Incorrect: The surgeon asks a question
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: The lights are adjusted by the circulator without touching the field
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Music plays in the OR

prompt: Gowns are sterile:

options.text: In the entire back including below the waist
In front from chest to sterile field level and sleeves to cuffs, per standard practice
Only at the neckline
Everywhere including after falling on the floor

explanation: Standard sterile gown areas are limited; backs and below-waist are unsterile.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: In the entire back including below the waist
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Everywhere including after falling on the floor

prompt: When a sterile team member must move past another, they should:

options.text: Brush backs together carelessly
Pass front-to-front or back-to-back while maintaining distance from nonsterile areas
Reach behind one another across the field
Remove one glove to shake hands

explanation: Passing technique protects gown fronts and the sterile field.

distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Reach behind one another across the field
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Remove one glove to shake hands

prompt: Strike-through on a sterile drape means:

options.text: The drape is more sterile
Moisture has penetrated, potentially contaminating the field
Electrosurgery is complete
The count is finished

explanation: Fluid strike-through is a contamination pathway.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: The drape is more sterile

prompt: Opening a peel pack onto the sterile field requires:

options.text: Peeling so the contents are projected uncontrolled onto the field
Presenting the item so the sterile person can take it without contaminating edges
Touching the inside of the pack with bare hands
Tearing through the middle of the package randomly

explanation: Edges are unsterile; transfer technique must protect sterility.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Peeling so the contents are projected uncontrolled onto the field
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Touching the inside of the pack with bare hands
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Tearing through the middle of the package randomly

prompt: The gallbladder is located in the:

options.text: Right upper quadrant, undersurface of the liver
Left lower quadrant only
Mediastinum
Popliteal fossa

explanation: Cholecystectomy addresses RUQ anatomy; know triangle of Calot concepts at high level.

prompt: The appendix typically arises from the:

options.text: Stomach fundus
Cecum
Sigmoid only always
Duodenal bulb

explanation: McBurney's point landmark for appendectomy positioning/incision planning.

prompt: Hernias involve:

options.text: Only bone fractures
Only skin rashes
Protrusion of tissue/organ through a defect in the cavity wall
Kidney stones exclusively

explanation: Know inguinal, femoral, umbilical, incisional types and mesh repair concepts.

prompt: The common bile duct drains bile into the:

options.text: Ileum directly
Bladder
Left atrium
Duodenum (via ampulla of Vater typically)

explanation: CBDE/ERCP anatomy relevant to biliary surgery complications.

prompt: Laparoscopic insufflation commonly uses:

options.text: CO2 to create pneumoperitoneum
100% nitrogen always only
Helium exclusively required
Room air preferred for fire safety always

explanation: CO2 is soluble/excretable; watch physiologic effects (hypercarbia, vagal responses).

prompt: The thyroid gland sits:

options.text: Inside the cranial vault
Anterior to the trachea in the neck
Posterior to the kidneys only
Within the scrotum

explanation: Recurrent laryngeal nerve and parathyroids are critical structures in thyroidectomy.

prompt: Wound classification 'Class III' generally indicates:

options.text: Clean elective under ideal conditions (Class I)
Clean-contaminated (Class II)
Contaminated (open/fresh accidental or major break in sterile technique/spillage)
Dirty/infected with old trauma/pus (Class IV)

explanation: Classification guides antibiotic expectations and SSI risk counseling.

prompt: Hemostasis methods include:

options.text: Only antibiotics
Only Trendelenburg
Only counting sponges without vessel control
Clamps, ligatures, electrosurgery, clips, and topical agents

explanation: CST anticipates suture ties, energy devices, and clip appliers.

distractorExplanations.c: Counts prevent retention not bleeding control alone.

prompt: The meniscus is found in the:

options.text: Knee joint
Eyeball lens only
Liver lobule
Middle ear ossicles only as 'meniscus'

explanation: Arthroscopic meniscectomy/repair is common ortho CST knowledge.

prompt: Dehiscence means:

options.text: Excessive scar keloid only
Separation of wound edges
Complete sterile technique
Normal primary intention healing without issue

explanation: Evisceration is protrusion of viscera—surgical emergency.

prompt: The spleen is located primarily in which abdominal quadrant?

options.text: Right lower quadrant
Left upper quadrant
Right upper quadrant only
Pelvis midline

explanation: The spleen lies in the LUQ beneath the diaphragm.

prompt: Which vessel is typically controlled within the mesoappendix during appendectomy?

options.text: Left gastric artery as the only vessel
Appendiceal artery within the mesoappendix
Aorta at the iliac bifurcation always
Pulmonary vein

explanation: The appendiceal artery supplies the appendix via the mesoappendix.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Left gastric artery as the only vessel
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Aorta at the iliac bifurcation always

prompt: The epididymis is located:

options.text: On the superior/posterior aspect of the testis
Inside the gallbladder fossa
Within the mediastinum
Only in the female pelvis

explanation: Epididymis sits along the testis and connects to the vas deferens.

distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Only in the female pelvis

prompt: During thyroid surgery, which nerve is at risk near the inferior thyroid artery territory?

options.text: Optic nerve
Recurrent laryngeal nerve
Phrenic nerve in the leg
Sciatic nerve

explanation: Recurrent laryngeal nerves are critical structures in thyroidectomy.

distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Phrenic nerve in the leg

prompt: The greater omentum is:

options.text: A double layer of peritoneum hanging from the stomach that can wall off infection
A bone in the wrist
The lining of the ventricle
A type of suture material

explanation: Greater omentum is a peritoneal apron from the greater curvature of the stomach.

distractorExplanations.b: Incorrect: A bone in the wrist
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: The lining of the ventricle
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: A type of suture material

prompt: Monopolar electrosurgery requires:

options.text: An active electrode and a dispersive (return) pad with good contact
No return pad ever
Alcohol pooling under the pad intentionally
Activation while touching the OR table metal with the active tip casually

explanation: Pad placement on well-vascularized muscle, avoid scars/implants/bony prominences per policy; prevent alternate site burns.

prompt: Bipolar electrosurgery current path is:

options.text: Through the dispersive pad always like monopolar
Between the two tines of the forceps—no dispersive pad required
Only through the anesthesia machine
Unable to coagulate vessels

explanation: Bipolar useful near nerves/wet fields with localized current.

prompt: Laser safety includes:

options.text: Staring into the beam to aim better
Alcohol prep puddles left during activation
Eyewear specific to wavelength, controlled access, and nonreflective precautions
Paper drapes ignited as a teaching tool

explanation: Fire/eye/plume hazards; use wet drapes/smoke evacuation as indicated.

prompt: Pneumatic tourniquet overinflation/time excess risks:

options.text: Improved sensation always
Mandatory hypothermia
Sterile field contamination automatically
Nerve injury, ischemia, and tissue damage

explanation: Document times; exsanguinate appropriately; notify team of duration.

prompt: Radiation safety for fluoroscopy uses:

options.text: Time, distance, and shielding (lead)
Standing closer to increase dose for better images as a safety rule
Lead aprons left outside always during cases
Holding continuous fluoro without reason

explanation: CST may wear dosimeters/aprons; hands out of beam.

prompt: A malfunctioning OR table should be:

options.text: Used if the case is already booked
Reported and removed from service until repaired—not used 'carefully'
Kicked to make it work
Covered with extra sheets to hide faults

explanation: Equipment failure can cause crush/fall/positioning injuries.

prompt: Surgical plume from electrosurgery/laser should be:

options.text: Inhaled freely as harmless steam always
Directed toward the sterile corridor for ventilation
Evacuated with appropriate smoke evacuation systems
Ignored per outdated myths

explanation: Plume can contain toxins/particles; PPE and evacuation protect staff.

prompt: Implantable electronic devices (e.g., pacemakers) require caution with:

options.text: Bipolar only ever banned in all cases worldwide
No energy devices ever for any surgery
MRI in the OR routinely midcase
Monopolar electrosurgery—coordinate with anesthesia/cardiology protocols

explanation: Use bipolar when possible, short bursts, pad placement strategies per protocol.

distractorExplanations.b: Energy often still needed with precautions.

prompt: Sharps safety on the field includes:

options.text: Neutral zone/no-hands passing techniques and proper sharps containers
Recapping suture needles with two hands toward the body
Hiding sharps in sponges
Leaving loaded needle holders facing up toward eyes casually

explanation: OSHA and AORN-aligned practices reduce sticks.

distractorExplanations.c: Sharps in sponges cause RSI/counts errors.

prompt: If a count is incorrect, the team should:

options.text: Close quickly to finish on time
Notify surgeon, recount, search wound/field/trash per policy, and obtain imaging if unresolved
Assume it fell on the floor and ignore
Alter count sheet to match without search

explanation: Never falsify counts; retained items are never events.

prompt: A grounding pad (patient return electrode) for monopolar electrosurgery should be:

options.text: Placed over a bony prominence with poor contact
Applied to clean, dry, well-vascularized skin with full contact per IFU
Cut in half to fit small patients routinely without approved pads
Omitted if the surgeon uses high power

explanation: Proper dispersive electrode placement prevents alternate-site burns.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Placed over a bony prominence with poor contact
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Cut in half to fit small patients routinely without approved pads
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Omitted if the surgeon uses high power

prompt: Which is a correct action if a surgical count is incorrect?

options.text: Close the wound immediately without notifying the team
Notify the surgeon, search systematically, and use imaging as directed by protocol
Hide the discrepancy from the record
Assume the missing sponge dissolved

explanation: Count discrepancies require immediate communication and resolution steps.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Close the wound immediately without notifying the team
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Hide the discrepancy from the record
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Assume the missing sponge dissolved

prompt: Harmonic/ultrasonic shears cut and coagulate using:

options.text: Ionizing radiation
High-frequency mechanical vibration that denatures protein
Only cold steel without energy
Compressed nitrogen alone

explanation: Ultrasonic devices use vibrating blades for cutting/coagulation.

distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Only cold steel without energy

prompt: When using a pneumatic tourniquet, staff must:

options.text: Ignore inflation time
Document tourniquet time and follow safe pressure/time limits
Place it over the incision site
Inflate without monitoring duration

explanation: Tourniquet pressure and duration are patient-safety critical parameters.

distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Place it over the incision site

prompt: Smoke plume from electrosurgery/laser should be:

options.text: Ignored because it is sterile air
Evacuated with appropriate smoke evacuation to reduce inhalation exposure
Directed toward the scrub team's faces
Used to inflate the abdomen

explanation: Surgical smoke contains hazardous particulates; evacuation protects the team.

distractorExplanations.a: Incorrect: Ignored because it is sterile air
distractorExplanations.c: Incorrect: Directed toward the scrub team's faces
distractorExplanations.d: Incorrect: Used to inflate the abdomen
